proof_trace: Two call sites in tool_args() assign ber_strdup() directly into passwd.bv_val and immediately call strlen(passwd.bv_val) with no intervening NULL check (L982→L990, L1476→L1477). ber_strdup returns NULL when ber_memalloc_x fails (OOM), making both strlen calls a confirmed NULL dereference path. No other enforcement mechanism exists in the function to guard these uses.
attack_scenario: If the system is under memory pressure when a user runs any OpenLDAP client tool with -w or interactive password prompt (-W), ber_strdup() returns NULL and strlen(passwd.bv_val) dereferences it, crashing the client process. An attacker who can cause memory exhaustion on the client host (e.g., through a co-located process or resource-limit manipulation) can reliably crash any LDAP tool invocation that uses password authentication.

proof_trace: `ber_strdup()` at L71 in `ldif_open_url` can return NULL on allocation failure, but the result `p` is passed without any NULL check to `ldap_pvt_hex_unescape(p)` at L80, which dereferences `*s` immediately at url.c:L1625 — causing a NULL pointer dereference crash. All other `ber_strdup` callers in libldap (ldif.c:L113, ldifutil.c:L637) do NULL-check the return value, confirming this is a coding defect, not intentional design.
attack_scenario: An attacker induces an OOM condition while an LDIF file containing an `include: file://path` directive is processed (e.g., via slapd config reload or ldapadd with crafted input); `ber_strdup` returns NULL, and the subsequent `ldap_pvt_hex_unescape(NULL)` at fetch.c:L80 dereferences NULL at url.c:L1625, crashing the LDAP server (DoS).

proof_trace: At line 71, `ber_strdup(urlstr)` can return NULL on OOM; there is no NULL check before line 80 unconditionally passes `p` to `ldap_pvt_hex_unescape(p)`, which immediately dereferences `*s` (url.c:1625), causing a NULL pointer dereference crash. On non-Unix platforms (`LDAP_DIRSEP != '/'`), `strchr(NULL, '/')` at line 76 is additionally triggered. The proof fails: the function has no guard protecting any use of `p` after allocation.
attack_scenario: An attacker who can trigger a low-memory condition (e.g., via concurrent requests exhausting heap) while a `file:` URL is processed through `ldif_fetch_url()` will cause `ber_strdup()` to return NULL; the subsequent unconditional `ldap_pvt_hex_unescape(NULL)` dereferences the NULL pointer, crashing the LDAP server process (DoS via SIGSEGV).

proof_trace: ber_strdup() at fetch.c:71 returns NULL on malloc failure; no NULL check exists before ldap_pvt_hex_unescape(p) is called at fetch.c:80. ldap_pvt_hex_unescape immediately dereferences its argument via `*s` in the for-loop at url.c:1625, causing a NULL pointer dereference (SIGSEGV).
attack_scenario: Under memory pressure, an attacker sends a `file://...` URL via LDIF `include:` directives or the URL-fetch API; if ber_strdup fails due to OOM, ldap_pvt_hex_unescape(NULL) crashes the LDAP server process (DoS).

proof_trace: ber_strdup(pw) at line 1476 assigns its return value directly to passwd.bv_val; line 1477 immediately calls strlen(passwd.bv_val) with no intervening NULL check. ber_strdup_x (memory.c:643) returns NULL when ber_memalloc_x fails, making this a NULL dereference if allocation fails. The if(pw) guard on line 1475 checks the input to ber_strdup, not its output.
attack_scenario: A user runs any LDAP client tool with -W (want_bindpw), enters a password, and the process is under memory pressure causing ber_memalloc_x to return NULL; strlen(NULL) then triggers a SIGSEGV, crashing the client process.
